title: RA.1003 XDB

translation.content: There's an old German proverb that goes "Der stete Tropfen höhlt den Stein." Literally, it means a constant dripping wears away the stone, but the point isn't about force but patience: slow, steady repetition can leave the deepest mark. It's an apt metaphor for the career of Kosta Athanassiadis, better known as XDB. Active since the early '90s, first as a DJ and then as a producer by the decade's end, Athanassiadis has built a career less on hype than persistence. His catalogue spans labels like Dial, Metrolux and Echocord, alongside a steady trickle of EPs and remixes that have quietly cemented his reputation as one of minimal house and techno's unsung heroes. That patience carries into his sound and production ethos. Where many of his peers embraced software upgrades and new workflows, Athanassiadis has long stuck to Cubase, a handful of trusty instruments and 30-year-old speakers he claims to have run "hundreds of thousands of tunes" through. He also still prefers to use inexpensive, straightforward gear—what matters, he insists, is not the tools but the feel. The result is a sound that's stripped back, direct and enduring. Lately, Athanassiadis has found himself back in focus. With minimal enjoying fresh attention, his calendar has filled, and with it a run of b2b sets—most often alongside PLO Man, a regular sparring partner this year. True to form, though, you won't find that his style has changed much. Over 30 years after his first gig, you can rest assured you'll still find him playing with patience, carving out long arcs rather than sharp peaks. His RA Mix captures Athanassiadis in a reflective mood. Running just over two hours, RA.1003 is a hushed yet absorbing affair, moving seamlessly from the delicate atmospherics of Valentino Mora and Caldera to the machine funk of Robert Hood, Solid Gold Playaz and Marcellus Pittman. There are left turns folded into XDB's patient narrative arc, too: John Carpenter's brooding scores here, DJ Sprinkles' melancholic work with Will Long on " Acid Trax N (Acid Dog)" there. It's the sound of a DJ who has been quietly chiseling away for three decades, and who understands the value of persistence as much as restraint. -Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Enjoying summertime. Been playing lots of outdoor festivals and some club gigs in between. Lately, I'm often in the UK and I love it! Mostly in London, but also Manchester, Liverpool and I've just come back from a fantastic weekend at Houghton Festival in Norfolk. There are also some new music ideas shaping up. Production wise, "Odican" came out in March on Forbidden Dance and it's included in the mix as my latest contribution to our small world of electronic music lovers. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? It was a late-night recording at my home. I used two Technics SL-1210MK2 turntables and a classic Rodec mixer. One take as always and the feel of spontaneous choices what to play next. It doesn't have to be perfect, just an authentic feel and not clinical. The idea was to start with one of my favourite producers in recent years and go with the flow from there and see where the journey leads to. After two and a half hours, I thought it'd be cool to end with John Carpenter's "The Duke Arrives" and decided to push the stop button on my Tascam recorder. Had a re-listen and it's super fine, so here you go. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm a fan of b2bs recently. I'm looking forward to playing with PLO Man once again, this time for the Clone Night at Club RAUM during ADE. But also super excited to close Basilar Festival with Ron Albrecht and to play again with one of my favourite turntable jockeys, Mr. Don Williams, this time in Berlin. What's one club or party which had a major impact on you as an artist? Freerotation in Wales. A quality music weekend. Every artist brings their absolute best there. Back in 2009, it's where Lakuti heard me play for the first time, and it was followed by an invitation to play London and join her artist booking roster where I stayed for 15 years until Uzuri closed a year ago. I'm happy to be part of the BomMaa agency artist roster now, based in Lisbon. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? We need to pay more attention to preparing for the future AI is shaping. The world hasn't realised yet that we're going to lose the battle against the machines in the long term.
translation.blurb: The German minimal DJ returns to the spotlight with two hours of artfully subtle house and techno.
